Definition: The process of identifying and ranking specific geographic areas for action to achieve the greatest conservation impact with limited resources
Purpose_problem_to solve: "The primary issue land-use planning attempts to solve is the allocation of a finite resources (space, financial, time) among competing demands, prioritizing biodiversity conservation."
How_predictions_inform_decisions: Model predictions tell planners where the highest-value habitats are., "Evaluating Alternative Future Scenarios (""What-If"" scenarios). Model predicitons can be used to quantify the ecological cost of each proposed zoning plan, allowing mutiple users to make transparent choices.", "Predictive models help multiple users to mathematically identify which areas might offer the highest ""return on investment"" for species, communities or biodiversity in general."
How_uncertainty_influence_decisions: "If the model has high commission errors (overpredicting habitat), it will affect potential areas for other uses and/or waist limited resources in areas of low value for conservation", "If the model has high omission errors (missing actual habitat), it will potentially missed the opoortunity to protect last habitat for the species (more sensitive to those rare or range-restricted species)."
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: Practitioners want to avoid searching for land or approving programs or implementing actions in areas where the predicted benefit or suitability doesn't actually exist, "If practitioners omit a sensitive ecosystem or remaining habitat for species at risk, that habitat may be permanently lost by human activities"
Reference_example: Loiselle et al. (2003)

Definition: The process of identifying and ranking specific geographic areas for action to achieve the greatest conservation impact with limited resources
Purpose_problem_to solve: Inappropriate habitat protection leads to higher extinction vulnerabilities.
How_predictions_inform_decisions: Identifying potential areas of high suitability aimed to protect species at risk
How_uncertainty_influence_decisions: "Uncertain suitable environments may lead to suboptimal reserve selection (e.g., erros of commision)."
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: This can result in the misallocation of limited economic resources and conservation efforts towards low-value regions., Practitioners want to minimize omission errors to ensure the protected area harbor all habitats that are vital for the survival of the species.
Reference_example: Di Marco et al. (2016); Velazco et al. (2020); Titeux et al. (2019)

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Model_application_subcategory: Improving habitat connectivity and reducing fragmentation
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: Practitioners want to minimize omissions to ensure that every viable pathway is considered. Fail to identify link between critical patches might compromise habitat connectivity for the species., Practitioners want to strictly reduce commission errors to ensure that the corridors they invest in are truly functional.

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "Practitioners want a precautionary approach. They want to ensure that no ""adverse effect"" is missed, even if the probability is low.", Practitioners want to avoid being forced to solve irrelevant issues that divert funds away from real environmental protection.
Reference_example: Dias et al. (2025); Baker et al. (2020)

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "Users prioritize the Critical Avoidance of omission errors to prevent extinction, while maintaining High Avoidance of commission errors to ensure the designation is legally and scientifically defensible."

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: The priorities center on identifying failures in the current system and ensuring that future management decisions are based on biological reality rather than paper protections.
Reference_example: Sundlad et al. (2010); Sousa et al. (2014); Syfert et al. (2014)

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "Users prioritize the Critical Avoidance of omission errors to ensure no ""silent extinctions"" occur. The species may reach a ""point of no return"" (an extinction vortex) before anyone realizes the model was wrong."
Reference_example: Zurell et al. (2023)

Definition:
Habitat evaluation is the systematic, quantitative assessment of a landscape’s capacity to support a specific species or ecological community.
Habitat evaluation focuses on the quality, configuration, and risk associated with the species landscape.
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "High Avoidance is prioritized for both types of errors in this specific use case. Errors in these models can lead to a ""false sense of progress"" (unrealized gains) or ""hidden destruction"" (unseen losses), both of which undermine conservation reporting and land management."
Reference_example: Paterson et al. (2024); Carvajal et al. (2018)

Definition: Sampling is an iterative cycle where existing model predictions actively dictate where field biologists should go next to gather the most valuable data.
What_practitioners_want: "Users want to minimize omission errors to ensure the model doesn't ""hide"" potential discovery sites."
Reference_example: Andrella et al. (2023); Eyre et al. (2022)

Definition: Sampling is an iterative cycle where existing model predictions actively dictate where field biologists should go next to gather the most valuable data.
What_practitioners_want: "Users want to minimize omissions to ensure that ""surprising"" populations are not ignored."
Reference_example: Lyons et al. (2020); Srivastava et. (2019); Gallien et al. (2012)

Definition: Sampling is an iterative cycle where existing model predictions actively dictate where field biologists should go next to gather the most valuable data.
What_practitioners_want: Users want to minimize omissions to ensure the survey is comprehensive and captures the full variation of the species' niche.
Reference_example: Peterman et al. (2013)

What_practitioners_want: "Users prioritize Critical Avoidance of commission errors to ensure a high Return on Investment. A commission error occurs when the model predicts a site is suitable for restoration, but the species fails to establish there after the work is done."
Reference_example: Silva et al. (2016)

Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "Users want to minimize omissions to ensure early detection and rapid response. If a model omits a high-risk areas of invasion, the species can establish itself unnoticed. By the time it is discovered, the window for control/eradication has usually closed, and the cost of management might increase substantially."
Reference_example: Lyons et al. (2020); Srivastava et. (2019); Gallien et al. (2012)

Purpose_problem_to solve: A new invader is likely to impact particular habitats.
How_predictions_inform_decisions: Predicting areas of potential occupancy to target surveillance and management.
How_uncertainty_influence_decisions: "Under-prediction may miss critical surveillance, over-prediction may waste management resources."
Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: "Users want to minimize mistakes that result in a ""game over"" scenario, that is the irreversible expansion of the invasive species beyond the point of human control."
Reference_example: Barbet et al. (2018); Jarnevich et al. (2018)

Spatial_extent: Global, Range-wide, Jurisdictional, Spatial management unit
What_practitioners_want: Fails to flag a vulnerable area as high risk. Missing a potential risk zone can lead to an irreversible ecological and economic disaster.
Reference_example: McCulloch et al. (2023);  Hui (2023)

What_practitioners_want: Users want Critical Avoidance of commission. They need the highest possible certainty that the destination can support the species for the long term.
Reference_example: Finn et al. (2024)

Model_application_subcategory: Future species range expansion and/or contractions
What_practitioners_want: "If a model omits these new areas, managers may not secure ""stepping stone"" corridors to help the species reach its future home. "
Reference_example: Elith et al. (2010); Soafer et al. (2018)

Model_application_subcategory: Identify the effect of climate change on species abundances
What_practitioners_want: Missing a decline prevents timely interventions
Reference_example: Howard et al. (2014); Waldock et al. (2021)

What_practitioners_want: "Users prioritize avoiding Omission errors to ensure they don't miss the last stands of a species (climate refugia), while High Avoidance of Commission is necessary to prevent investing in sink habitats that will become unsuitable before restoration can take hold."
Reference_example: Stralberg et al. (2025); Leston et al. (2024)

Model_application_subcategory: Identify potential species and ecosystems distributional shifts
What_practitioners_want: Fails to detect a tipping point or regime shift.
User want to avoid implementing actions in areas where species of concern are most likely present
Priority given to errors that lead to overharvesting.
Reference_example: Hart et al. (2025); Paradinas et al. (2023)

Model_application_subcategory: Using SDMS to calculate biodiversity metrics to inform global and national targets (Species habitat Index)
What_practitioners_want: Omissions lead to a systematic underestimation of global biodiversity loss.
Reference_example: Karimi et al. (2025); Dansereau et al. (2022)

What_practitioners_want: "Users want to identify which specific errors occur and why they occur (e.g., is the model over-fitting or under-fitting?)"
Reference_example: Foster et al. (2022); Rousseau & Betts (2022)

What_practitioners_want: "The primary priority is avoiding Omission errors that would miss the small, isolated refugia that were critical for the species' survival during past climate extremes."
Reference_example: Hodel et al. (2022); Regos et al. (2018)

Model_application_subcategory: Identify causal factors, variable importance, biotic interactions
What_practitioners_want: "Avoid commission: vital for ensuring the model isn't just ""predicting everywhere,"" which would invalidate the specificity of the ecological hypothesis. Avoid omission: necessary to ensure the hypothesis isn't prematurely rejected due to poor model sensitivity."

Model_application_subcategory: Trend estimation, population assessment, range estimation
What_practitioners_want: "Priority given to errors that hide a decline. In population management, the cost of being too optimistic is usually extinction."
Reference_example: Sen et al. (2024); Oliver et al. (2012); Knight et al. (2021); Torres et al. (2012)
